request.question: What type of criminals, named after a term from the Revolutionary War, were involved in the Clay County Savings Association Building robbery?

title: Clay County Savings Association Building
Edina Double Square Historic District
Bushwhacker
Clay County Courthouse (Alabama)
Claycomo, Missouri
Savings Bank Building
Old Clay County Courthouse (Clay, West Virginia)
Onondaga County Savings Bank
Clay County, Tennessee
Clay County Courthouse (Illinois)
sentences: The Clay County Savings Association in Liberty, Missouri, was robbed on February 13, 1866, making it one of the earliest documented daylight bank robberies.
The robbers escaped with at least $60,000 ( ) and killed a bystander outside.
The robbery is believed to have been conducted by a group of former Confederate guerrillas, possibly led by Jesse James or Archie Clement, which became known as the James–Younger Gang.
Edina Double Square Historic District is a national historic district located at Edina, Knox County, Missouri.
The district encompasses 37 contributing buildings in the central business district of Edina.
It developed between about 1865 and 1945 and includes representative examples of Italianate and Streamline Moderne style architecture.

Notable contributing buildings include the Public Works Administration funded Knox County Courthouse (1934-1935) designed by William B. Ittner, Bishoff Bakery (1891), Northern Hotel (1860s), Ennis House/Northern Hotel (c. 1865), Edina School and Gymnasium (1915-1916), D. H. Mudd Building (c. 1904), Phillip Linville Building (c. 1908, c. 1963), Tobias J. Lycan Building (c. 1891), Jacob Pugh Building (c. 1891), Albert G. Bostick Building (c. 1891), Knox County Savings Bank (c. 1891), Thomas Burk Buildings (c. 1881), Bank of Edina Building (c. 1907), Joseph F. Biggerstaff Buildings (c. 1881), Stablein Building (c. 1870), and Knox County Public Library (c. 1915).

Bushwhacking was a form of guerrilla warfare common during the American Revolutionary War, American Civil War and other conflicts in which there were large areas of contested land and few governmental resources to control these tracts.
This was particularly prevalent in rural areas during the Civil War where there were sharp divisions between those favoring the Union and Confederacy in the conflict.
The perpetrators of the attacks were called bushwhackers.
The term "bushwhacking" is still in use today to describe ambushes done with the aim of attrition.
The Clay County Courthouse is a historic courthouse building in Ashland, Clay County, Alabama.
The Classical Revival-style building has served as the county courthouse since its completion in 1906.
Notable characteristics include its large dome, with clocks inset on four sides, and cupola surmounted by a statuary representing justice.
It was added to the National Register of Historic Places on November 21, 1976.
Claycomo is a village in Clay County, Missouri, United States.
The village’s name is a portmanteau of "Clay, Co" (Clay County), and "Mo" (Missouri).
The population was 1,430 at the 2010 census.
Ford Motor Company has an assembly plant in Claycomo.
Kansas City Assembly is responsible for building the Ford F-150 pickup trucks and the Ford Transit vans.
The Savings Bank Building is a commercial building located at 101 South Front Street in Marquette, Michigan.
It is also known as the Marquette County Savings Bank.
The building was designated a Michigan State Historic Site in 1976 and listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1978.
The Old Clay County Courthouse in Clay, West Virginia was designed by Frank L. Packard and built in 1902.
The Beaux-Arts building was located on a hill overlooking the county seat.

The courthouse was the site of three notable trials: the Sarah Ann Legg trial of 1905, the first trial of a woman in Clay County for murder, the Booger Hole trial of 1917, in which citizens nearly lynched the defendants, and the Oscar Bail trial of 1953, in which Bail was convicted of killing a mine guard in the Great Widen Coal Strike.
The Onondaga County Savings Bank was chartered in 1855 in Syracuse, New York.
It was a franchise ahead of its time and had four separate branches by the late 19th century.
The "Onondaga County Savings Bank Building" was constructed in 1896 adjacent to the Erie Canal in "Clinton Square".
Clay County is a county located in the U.S. state of Tennessee.
As of the 2010 census, the population was 7,861.
Its county seat and only incorporated city is Celina.
Clay County is named in honor of American statesman Henry Clay, member of the United States Senate from Kentucky and United States Secretary of State in the 19th century.
Its current mayor is Dale Reagan.
The Clay County Courthouse, located at 300 Broadway Street in Louisville, is the county courthouse serving Clay County, Illinois.
Built in 1913, the courthouse was Clay County's fourth courthouse; it has served continuously as the seat of Clay County government since its opening.
Architect Joseph W. Royer, who planned several other Illinois courthouses, designed the Classical Revival building.
The courthouse was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 2015.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: What type of criminals, named after a term from the Revolutionary War, were involved in the Clay County Savings Association Building robbery?
hop1_docs: Clay County Savings Association Building | The Clay County Savings Association in Liberty, Missouri, was robbed on February 13, 1866, making it one of the earliest documented daylight bank robberies.  The robbers escaped with at least $60,000 ( ) and killed a bystander outside.  The robbery is believed to have been conducted by a group of former Confederate guerrillas, possibly led by Jesse James or Archie Clement, which became known as the James–Younger Gang.

Aryan Republican Army | The Aryan Republican Army is the name given to white supremacist criminal group active in the United States in the early to mid-1990s.  The group is alleged to have associated with convicted bomber Timothy McVeigh in the months before the Oklahoma City bombing.  The organization was sometimes referenced in the media as the Midwest Bank Robbers.  The group was created by Peter Langan and Richard Lee Guthrie in 1992.  The group considered themselves a leaderless organization, meaning that there was no true leader to the group and that they were more of a group of associated people acting together to achieve their goals.  Although the group was mainly a criminal enterprise, they did have a terrorist like agenda.  With the money they were able to steal in the 22 confirmed robberies they were involved in, the group began stockpiling weapons and ammunition.  It is believed that the group wished to start a race war with the weapons that were stockpiled.  The group began to fall apart as members of the Aryan Republican Army were arrested after one of the former members of the group became an informant as part of a plea bargain.  By mid-1996, practically all known members had been apprehended and the group was in shambles.

State Line Mob | The State Line Mob was an association of criminal elements that operated in the 1950s and 1960s at the Mississippi–Tennessee state line in Alcorn County, Mississippi, and McNairy County, Tennessee, along U.S. Route 45.  The State Line Mob was involved in bootlegging, gambling, prostitution, tourist fleecing, robbery, and murder.  A few of the members were from Phenix City, Alabama, having been displaced from that town when martial law was declared by the Governor and the Alabama National Guard attempted to clean the town up.

Florencia 13 | The Florencia 13 is an American criminal street gang in Los Angeles, California, composed mainly of Hispanic-Americans and some blacks.  The gangs name is after the Florence area of Los Angeles county, the 13 is for it's allegiance to the Mexican Mafia.  They are involved in drug smuggling, murder, assault, robbery etc.

Arifs (gang) | The Arifs are a South East London-based Turkish Cypriot criminal organization heavily involved in armed robbery, contract killing, drug trafficking and other racketeering-related activities within London's underworld since the late 1960s.  Following the downfall of the Kray brothers, the Arifs were one of several criminal organizations who took control of the London underworld including the Clerkenwell crime syndicate and the Brindle family with whom they were engaged in a highly publicized gangland war during the 1990s.

Quaker Springs, New York | Quaker Springs is a hamlet in Saratoga County.  The zip code is 12871.  Quaker Springs is between NY-32 Route 4.  Quaker Springs United Methodist Church is located in the hamlet.  An anti-slavery society was based in Quaker Springs and several possible participants in the Underground Railroad were in Quaker Springs.  The name Quaker Springs came from a type of mineral spring located in the area.  The area including Quaker Springs was involved in Revolutionary War positions.
U.S. intelligence involvement with German and Japanese war criminals after World War II | U.S. intelligence involvement with German and Japanese war criminals after World War II
hop1_titles: U.S. intelligence involvement with German and Japanese war criminals after World War II
response.summary_1:
Primary topic: Clay County Savings Association robbery and suspected perpetrators.

- Passage facts: the Clay County Savings Association "was robbed on February 13, 1866"; robbers "escaped with at least $60,000"; robbery "is believed to have been conducted by a group of former Confederate guerrillas"; "possibly led by Jesse James or Archie Clement"; the group "became known as the James–Younger Gang."
- Missing / ambiguous: the passages do NOT state a connection to any "term from the Revolutionary War" (the required term is absent). Candidate bridging terms to check (ambiguous): "bushwhackers", "guerrillas", "partisans", "jayhawkers".
- Prioritized next-hop retrievals:
  1. James–Younger Gang — fetch lede/infobox and "Origins" or "Members" sections (look for labels like "bushwhackers" or other historical epithets).
  2. Jesse James — fetch lede/biography and "Early life" / "Civil War service" sections (mentions of guerrilla/bushwhacker labels).
  3. "Bushwhacker" (or "bushwhackers") — fetch etymology/history and Civil War usage (to confirm Revolutionary War origin/usage).
  4. "Guerrilla" (etymology) or "Partisan (American Revolution)" — fetch etymology/history sections if needed to confirm which term originates with the Revolutionary War.
hop2_docs: Jesse James | Jesse Woodson James (September 5, 1847April 3, 1882) was an American outlaw, bank and train robber, guerrilla, and leader of the James–Younger Gang.  Raised in the "Little Dixie" area of western Missouri, James and his family maintained strong Southern sympathies.  He and his brother Frank James joined pro-Confederate guerrillas known as "bushwhackers" operating in Missouri and Kansas during the American Civil War.  As followers of William Quantrill and "Bloody Bill" Anderson, they were accused of participating in atrocities against Union soldiers and civilian abolitionists, including the Centralia Massacre in 1864.

James–Younger Gang | The James–Younger Gang was a notable 19th-century gang of American outlaws that centered around Jesse James and his brother Frank James.  The gang was based in the state of Missouri, the home of most of the members.

The Great Northfield Minnesota Raid | The Great Northfield Minnesota Raid is a 1972 Technicolor Western film about the James-Younger Gang distributed by Universal Pictures.  It was directed by Philip Kaufman in a cinéma vérité style and starred Cliff Robertson as Cole Younger, Robert Duvall as Jesse James, Luke Askew as Jim Younger, R. G. Armstrong as Clell Miller, John Pearce as Frank James, and Matt Clark as Bob Younger.  The film purports to recreate the James-Younger Gang's most infamous escapade, the September 7, 1876, robbery of "the biggest bank west of the Mississippi" in Northfield, Minnesota.
Jim Younger | James Hardin Younger (January 15, 1848 – October 19, 1902) was a notable American outlaw and member of the James–Younger Gang.  He was the brother of Cole, John and Bob Younger

Cole Younger | Thomas Coleman "Cole" Younger (January 15, 1844 – March 21, 1916) was an American Confederate guerrilla during the American Civil War and later an outlaw leader with the James–Younger Gang.  He was the eldest brother of Jim, John and Bob Younger, who were also members of the gang.

John Younger | John Harrison Younger (1851 – March 17, 1874) was an American outlaw, the brother of Cole, Jim and Bob.  He was briefly a member of the James–Younger Gang, a band of outlaws that also included the infamous Jesse James.

Bob Younger | Robert Ewing "Bob" Younger (October 29, 1853 – September 16, 1889) was an American criminal and outlaw, the younger brother of Cole, Jim and John Younger.  He was a member of the James–Younger Gang.
response.summary_2: The robbery was carried out by former Confederate guerrillas known as "bushwhackers." Jesse James was an American outlaw who, with his brother, joined pro-Confederate guerrillas called "bushwhackers" operating in Missouri and Kansas during the Civil War.
